topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment felt thick, like unmoving molasses. Leo stared at the half-finished manuscript spread across the coffee table, the pages filled with his own idiosyncrasies, his irritating habits, the way he always left his socks draped over the armchair. A low, simmering annoyance tightened his jaw. He’d been trying to focus on assembling flat-pack furniture all afternoon, but every time his eyes drifted to the manuscript, a strange, prickly feeling surfaced. He picked up a stray bookmark—a ribbon he’d forgotten he’d placed there—and smoothed it out. It was a chapter titled, "The Perpetual Sigh." A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed to vibrate with sheer exasperation. He felt a distinct urge to shove the book away, to bury the subject beneath a pile of unopened mail.

[story 2]

“Honestly, the sheer audacity of it all,” Sarah muttered, rubbing the bridge of her nose as she surveyed the desk. The words detailing her morning routine—the precise way she brewed her tea, the precise shade of blue she chose for her work blouse—were laid out in elegant prose. A tightness gripped her chest, making her breath shallow. She paced three steps to the window, watching the rain streak down the glass. It was ridiculous. She hated feeling scrutinized, even by the person she loved most. When she finally turned back to the page, the meticulous description of her stubborn refusal to admit she was wrong made her clench her fists. She wanted to slam the laptop shut, to declare the whole endeavor absurd and pointless.

[story 3]

Mark leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed tightly over his chest. The air in the living room seemed too warm, too heavy for his liking. He watched Alex meticulously edit a passage describing his tendency to lose his keys in increasingly illogical places. A deep furrow settled between his brows. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was a profound sense of being burdened by an unwelcome, detailed observation. He felt a distinct desire to retreat to his room, to find a space where the narrative wouldn't exist. When Alex looked up, catching him watching, Mark just offered a tight, dismissive nod, the gesture conveying volumes of internal displeasure without uttering a single complaint.

Leo stared at the half-finished manuscript spread across the coffee table, the pages filled with his own idiosyncrasies, his irritating habits, the way he always left his socks draped over the armchair. A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed to vibrate with sheer exasperation. He felt a distinct urge to shove the book away, to bury the subject beneath a pile of unopened mail.

“Honestly, the sheer audacity of it all,” Sarah muttered, rubbing the bridge of her nose as she surveyed the desk. The words detailing her morning routine—the precise way she brewed her tea, the precise shade of blue she chose for her work blouse—were laid out in elegant prose. Mark leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed tightly over his chest. The air in the living room seemed too warm, too heavy for his liking. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was a profound sense of being burdened by an unwelcome, detailed observation.
